one_line_takeaway: By perturbing the embeddings of a prompt's key entity and reading off KL divergence and token-probability shifts in the model's own output distribution, SHINE distinguishes fabricated text (weak entity association) from misaligned text (contradicts known-but-misapplied knowledge) without external knowledge, fine-tuning, or labeled training data, beating seven baselines on hallucination detection across four LLMs and four datasets.
key_finding: Fabricated text shows a much lower mean KL divergence after entity perturbation (0.21) than aligned (0.92) or misaligned (1.36) text, indicating fabricated tokens are weakly tied to the perturbed entity's representation. Misaligned text shows a positive mean Delta P (0.03) -- generation probability rises after perturbation -- versus negative Delta P for aligned (-0.16) and near-zero for fabricated (-0.03) text. On the automated SHINE pipeline (Table 1 confusion matrices), for LLaMA2 the Model Knowledge Test correctly identifies 88.46% of fabricated NEC examples and 86.15% of fabricated Biography examples; the Alignment Test then correctly classifies 75.53%/73.53% of aligned/misaligned NEC examples and 81.82%/87.27% of aligned/misaligned Biography examples. In binary hallucination detection (Table 2, NEC dataset, LLaMA2), SHINE correctly classifies 91.21% of fabricated text as hallucinated, versus 17.58% for SelfCheckGPT and 62.09% for BSDetector, and SHINE achieves the highest mean accuracy across all models/datasets compared against seven baselines: SelfCheckGPT, BSDetector, NLL, entropy, Hallucination Score, Semantic Entropy, and MARS(+SE). On efficiency, SelfCheckGPT takes 60.44 seconds per NEC example on an NVIDIA A10G GPU versus under 2 seconds for SHINE's Alignment Test (over 30x faster), and even at low keff (reduced SelfCheckGPT usage) SHINE still outperforms BSDetector, the strongest baseline.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'hallucination probing', a new task that classifies LLM-generated text into three fine-grained categories -- aligned (LLM has knowledge and responds faithfully), misaligned (LLM has knowledge but generates contradictions), and fabricated (LLM lacks relevant knowledge) -- refining the usual binary hallucinated/not-hallucinated framing. Building on a new empirical finding they call 'Entity Perturbation Impact' (perturbing the embeddings of a prompt's key entity with Gaussian noise and measuring the resulting KL divergence and change in generation probability at each output token, Delta P_i), the authors show these two signals behave differently for aligned, misaligned, and fabricated text. They then build SHINE (Systematic Hallucination Inspection with Noisy Entity), a two-stage, training-free, external-knowledge-free method: a Model Knowledge Test (KL-divergence-based) that flags fabricated text, followed by an Alignment Test (Delta P_i-based, with SelfCheckGPT invoked only for near-zero-signal cases) that separates aligned from misaligned text.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is not an adversarial/security paper. There is no attacker, no adversarial objective, and no threat model in the paper. The subject is model faithfulness/hallucination diagnosis for a benign user querying an LLM; SHINE probes the model's own internal knowledge signals (attention and generation likelihood at entity tokens) rather than defending against any adversary.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) the three-way taxonomy (aligned/misaligned/fabricated) could itself be further subdivided (e.g., misaligned text split by cause -- randomness vs. token-position dependency); (2) SHINE targets hallucinations caused by erroneous retrieval of knowledge about key entities specifically, and does not cover other hallucination types such as input-conflicting or context-conflicting hallucinations, nor non-entity-centric prompts like arithmetic or reasoning problems; (3) the approach focuses on entity keywords, leaving extension to non-entity keywords as future work. The authors also flag a 'Potential Risks' concern: SHINE uses the LLM itself as a proxy knowledge base, so its faithfulness judgments inherit any outdated or inaccurate knowledge the LLM holds, requiring caution before deploying its outputs in practical settings.

datasets_used: NEC (extended with trinary aligned/misaligned/fabricated labels from Liu et al. 2024a's entity-based benchmark; 359 responses per label class for LLaMA2, 359 for LLaMA3, 476 for Mistral)
Biography (extended with trinary labels from Min et al. 2023's FActScore real/fake biographies; 109/109/129 aligned/misaligned/fabricated for LLaMA2, 174/174/174 for LLaMA3, 128/128/128 for Mistral)
HaluEval (Li et al. 2023; 10,000 faithful and 10,000 hallucinated question-context-response tuples)
TriviaQA (Joshi et al. 2017; 257-268 faithful and 318 hallucinated examples per model, verified against Wikipedia)
quantitative_results: Entity Perturbation Impact pilot study (12 examples/class): mean KL divergence 0.92 (aligned), 1.36 (misaligned), 0.21 (fabricated); mean Delta P -0.16 (aligned), 0.03 (misaligned), -0.03 (fabricated)

SHINE probing confusion matrix, LLaMA2: fabricated correctly detected 88.46% (NEC) / 86.15% (Biography); aligned correctly classified 75.53% (NEC) / 81.82% (Biography); misaligned correctly classified 73.53% (NEC) / 87.27% (Biography)
Binary detection accuracy on fabricated text, NEC/LLaMA2: SHINE 91.21% vs. SelfCheckGPT 17.58% vs. BSDetector 62.09%
SHINE achieves the highest overall/mean detection accuracy across all models and datasets in Tables 2 and 3 (NEC, Biography, HaluEval, TriviaQA; LLaMA2, LLaMA3, Mistral, Qwen2.5)
Runtime: SelfCheckGPT 60.44s per NEC example (LLaMA2, NVIDIA A10G) vs. SHINE Alignment Test under 2s (>30x faster)
